development_classification.notes: Argentina is classified as a middle-income, Non-OECD country (Second World category). The Rincón and Sal de Vida lithium projects are located in remote, high-altitude Puna region (Salta and Catamarca provinces respectively). For HSESC compliance, this classification means heightened scrutiny is required around: indigenous community rights and free prior informed consultation (as evidenced by the July 2025 Olacapato public hearing for Rincón), environmental impact assessment requirements under provincial and national Argentine law, water management in the arid Puna environment, worker health and safety at elevations of 3,660–4,100 m asl, and supply chain due diligence given Argentina's evolving regulatory framework including the newly implemented RIGI incentive regime. IFC Performance Standards and Equator Principles are likely applicable given multilateral financing involvement (IDB Invest noted for Sal de Vida). Local labour standards, community grievance mechanisms, and biodiversity protection in the high-altitude wetland/salar ecosystems are key HSESC risk areas.

legislation.name: Argentine Constitution — Article 14 bis
legislation.notes: Constitutional foundation establishing the right to work in safe and healthy conditions as a fundamental right. Applies across all territory including Puna region mining operations.

legislation.name: Law No. 19,587 — Occupational Hygiene and Safety Law (Ley de Higiene y Seguridad en el Trabajo)
legislation.notes: Core national OSH law applicable to all establishments and operations across the entire territory of Argentina, including Rincon (Salta) and Sal de Vida (Puna region). Article 5(h) expressly addresses tasks that are very difficult, hazardous, or determinants of premature aging or exhaustion and/or performed in unhealthy places or environments — directly applicable to high-altitude remote Puna mine sites (3,400–4,600 m ASL). Supplemented by Decree 351/79 and Decree 1338/96, though Decree 351/79 was superseded for mining by Decree 249/2007.

legislation.name: Decree No. 351/1979 — Regulatory Decree of Law 19,587
legislation.notes: Regulatory decree implementing Law 19,587. Covers physical environmental conditions, ventilation, thermal load, pressure, humidity, lighting and chemical exposure limits. Superseded for mining activities by Decree 249/2007, except where Decree 249/2007 expressly refers back to it. Limited residual applicability to Rincon and Sal de Vida operations.

legislation.name: Law No. 24,557 — Occupational Risks Law (Ley de Riesgos del Trabajo — LRT)
legislation.notes: Primary legal framework for workplace health and safety risk management and compensation in Argentina. Mandates employers to secure coverage from Occupational Risk Insurers (ART). ARTs are responsible for prevention, workplace hazard management, and compensation for occupational illnesses. Employers must notify the ART within 24–48 hours of a workplace accident. Operates a no-fault compensation system. Applies to all mining operations including Rincon and Sal de Vida. Secondary source: https://servicios.infoleg.gob.ar/infolegInternet/verNorma.do?id=27971

legislation.name: Decree No. 249/2007 — Reglamento de Higiene y Seguridad para la Actividad Minera (Mining Hygiene and Safety Regulation)
legislation.notes: The single most important OSH instrument for Rincon (Salta) and all Puna mining operations including Sal de Vida. Sanctioned 20 March 2007, published in Boletín Oficial No. 31122 on 23 March 2007. Applicable to all mining activity as described in Articles 2 to 5 of the Mining Code, mandatory across the entire territory of Argentina. Supersedes Decree 351/79 for mining activities except where expressly referenced. Contains 191 articles across 7 titles covering underground mining, open-cut mining, and radioactive material mining. Covers general duties, occupational hygiene and safety services, occupational medical services, Joint Health and Safety Committees, training, general prevention measures, infrastructure services, first aid, contaminants, installations, machines and equipment, signage, explosives, electrical installations, and transport of personnel. SRT is the national enforcement authority. Recognises special characteristics of mining risks and environments. Additional sources: https://servicios.infoleg.gob.ar/infolegInternet/verNorma.do?id=126637 and https://www.aomaosam.org.ar/aoma/pdf/decreto-249-07.pdf

osh_legislation.gap_summary: No specific standalone national legislation exists for lithium mining in Argentina; lithium is governed by the general mining regulatory regime under Decree 249/2007 and Law 19,587. The research findings do not identify specific provisions addressing high-altitude physiological risks (e.g., acute mountain sickness, hypoxia) at Puna elevations of 3,400–4,600 m ASL within the national framework, nor brine-specific or lithium-specific chemical exposure standards. Provincial OSH and environmental layers from Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca add procedural requirements but are not detailed in the findings. No global frameworks (e.g., ILO conventions, ISO 45001) are referenced or confirmed as formally applied in the source material provided. The research findings do not address remote-site emergency response, helicopter evacuation protocols, or telecommunications requirements specific to Puna isolation conditions.

legislation.name: Argentine National Constitution — Articles 41 & 124 (Reformed 1994)
legislation.notes: Article 124 grants provinces original ownership of natural resources; Article 41 establishes minimum national environmental protection standards. Bedrock of mining and environmental governance in Argentina.

legislation.name: Argentine Mining Code (AMC) — National Law 1919 (as amended)
legislation.notes: Foundational mining legislation applicable to all mining activities including lithium. Amended by Laws 24,196, 24,224, 24,228, 24,498, and 24,585. Provinces regulate procedures in accordance with the Code.

legislation.name: Law 24,585 — Environmental Protection for Mining Activity
legislation.notes: Amended the Mining Code to incorporate environmental protection provisions. Requires submission of an Environmental Impact Report (EIR) prior to each phase of mining activity (prospecting, exploration, exploitation). Provincial authority issues an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) valid for two years, with mandatory updates every two years. Applies to Rincon (Salta) and Sal de Vida (Puna region) lithium projects.

legislation.name: General Environmental Law 25,675
legislation.notes: One of the two most relevant minimum environmental standards laws for the mining industry. Requires environmental insurance for any company conducting activities that might involve risk to the environment. Applies to lithium mining projects in the Puna region.

legislation.name: Law 24,051 — Hazardous Waste Management
legislation.notes: Regulates generation, transportation, storage, and disposal of hazardous waste. Sets standards for industrial waste management to prevent pollution and harm to human health. Relevant to lithium brine extraction operations.

legislation.name: National Law 25,688 — Regime of Environmental Management of Water
legislation.notes: Regulates water resources at the federal level. Particularly complex in application to lithium brine mining as brines are legally considered both water and mineral resources, creating regulatory overlap and an 'administrative labyrinth' in Argentina.

legislation.name: Glacier Protection Law 26,639 — Minimum Standards for Preservation of Glaciers and Periglacial Environments
legislation.regulator: IANIGLA (Argentine Institute for Snow, Ice and Environmental Sciences) / Provincial Governments (post-2026 reform)
legislation.notes: Prohibits mining exploration and exploitation activities that negatively affect glaciers and periglacial environments. Critically relevant to Puna region lithium projects (Sal de Vida, Rincon) given their high-altitude location. A 2026 reform shifted decision-making power from IANIGLA to provincial governments, raising concerns about inconsistent environmental oversight. Glacial reserves in northwest Argentina have shrunk by 17% in the last decade.

legislation.name: Federal Mining Agreement — Law 24,228
legislation.notes: Part of the suite of laws amending the Argentine Mining Code. Establishes federal-provincial coordination framework for mining regulation.

legislation.name: Mining Investment Law — Law 24,196
legislation.notes: Part of the suite of laws amending the Argentine Mining Code. Governs investment conditions for mining activities in Argentina.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: Minimum Environmental Standards Legislation (National Minimum Standards Framework)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: Argentina's national minimum environmental standards framework — comprising General Environmental Law 25,675 and Glacier Protection Law 26,639 — applies to all mining activities including lithium extraction at Rincon and Sal de Vida. Provinces must meet or exceed these standards.

environmental_legislation.gap_summary: Several significant regulatory gaps and complexities are identified: (1) Dual legal classification of lithium brines as both water and mineral resources creates jurisdictional overlap between mining and water law, resulting in regulatory ambiguity and an 'administrative labyrinth'. (2) The 2026 reform of the Glacier Protection Law decentralises glacier protection oversight from the national scientific body (IANIGLA) to provincial governments, raising concerns about inconsistent environmental oversight in the Puna region where lithium projects are concentrated. (3) While provinces are empowered to enact complementary environmental regulation, the research findings do not detail specific Salta or Jujuy/Catamarca provincial legislation applicable to Rincon and Sal de Vida beyond the national framework, representing an information gap. (4) The findings are truncated and may not capture the full 2026 glacier law reform provisions. (5) No specific indigenous consultation or Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) framework legislation is referenced, despite the Puna region having significant indigenous communities. (6) Water allocation frameworks specific to brine extraction in the salt pans remain legally ambiguous, with provincial water codes (Salta, Jujuy) insufficiently differentiated from standard water use regulations.

legislation.name: ILO Convention No. 169 (Indigenous and Tribal Peoples Convention) — Law 24,071
legislation.notes: Ratified by Argentina in 1992 via Law 24,071; entered into force July 2001. Requires Free Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) before projects affecting indigenous territories and resources. Consistently invoked by Puna communities in the context of lithium mining at Rincón and Sal de Vida. Communities have criticized consultation processes as non-compliant with ILO 169 intercultural requirements.

legislation.name: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)
legislation.notes: Argentina voted in support of UNDRIP. Recognizes indigenous rights to communal property, self-determination, and decision-making authority over lands and resources. Violations of Articles 3, 4, 19, and 26 alleged in context of provincial constitutional reforms and lithium exploitation without FPIC.

legislation.name: National Constitution of Argentina — Article 75, Section 17 (1994 Reform)
legislation.notes: Primary domestic source of indigenous rights. Mandates recognition of indigenous ethnic and cultural pre-existence, community land ownership, legal capacity of communities, and participation in decisions related to natural resources. Argentine courts have applied this article to require indigenous participation in mining decisions affecting land and way of life.

legislation.name: National Mining Code (Law 1919, as amended)
legislation.regulator: Secretariat of Mining / Provincial Mining Authorities
legislation.notes: Governs rights, obligations and procedures for exploration, exploitation and use of mineral substances in Argentina. Sets the legal framework between the state and mining operators. Does not prescribe community consultation or social benefit-sharing requirements beyond EIA — the community/social layer is left to provinces and international instruments.

legislation.name: Law 24,585 — Environmental Amendment to the Mining Code
legislation.regulator: Secretariat of Mining / Provincial Environmental Authorities
legislation.notes: Introduced environmental obligations for mining operators including mandatory Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) submission before initiating any activity, and requirement to mitigate or restore damages caused by mining activities. Does not specifically address community consultation or FPIC.

legislation.name: General Environmental Law (Law 25,675)
legislation.notes: Federal minimum environmental standard. Mandates public participation as a mandatory stage during EIA approval for projects with significant adverse environmental effects. Requires provinces to institutionalize consultation procedures or public hearings prior to authorization of natural resource exploitation projects. Applicable to lithium mining at Rincón and Sal de Vida.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: ILO Convention No. 169 — Free Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: Indigenous communities in the Puna region, including those near Salinas Grandes and Laguna de Guayatayoc spanning Jujuy and Salta, have consistently invoked ILO 169 and FPIC requirements in challenges to lithium mining projects. Communities have criticized existing consultation processes as non-compliant with ILO 169 intercultural requirements. A 2019 legal action filed by these communities with FARN reached the Argentine Supreme Court, which declared itself competent to intervene on grounds including violations of the right to consultation and FPIC.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: Argentina recognized indigenous community rights to communal property and self-determination under UNDRIP. Violations of Articles 3, 4, 19, and 26 have been alleged in connection with provincial constitutional reforms implemented within the framework of national lithium exploitation policy, carried out without FPIC. Advocacy was brought before the 57th UN Human Rights Council session.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: Inter-American Human Rights System (American Convention on Human Rights / Inter-American Court of Human Rights)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: In 2020, the Inter-American Court of Human Rights ordered the Argentine government to strengthen legal protections for indigenous communities in the Puna region. Communities from the Salinas Grandes and Laguna de Guayatayoc basin filed an action alleging violations of collective rights including consultation and FPIC in connection with lithium and borate mining projects, which subsequently triggered Argentine Supreme Court intervention.

community_social_legislation.gap_summary: Argentina lacks a single dedicated community and social legislation law for mining projects. The regulatory framework is fragmented across international treaties, the federal constitution, and provincial legislation, with no unified national law specifically governing FPIC, community benefit-sharing, or social impact assessment for lithium mining. The National Mining Code (Law 1919) and its environmental amendment (Law 24,585) do not prescribe community consultation or social benefit-sharing requirements beyond EIA, leaving these obligations to be inferred from international instruments (ILO 169, UNDRIP) and Article 75(17) of the Constitution. Provincial authorities hold primary jurisdiction over consultation processes, leading to inconsistent application and enforcement across Salta and Catamarca. Indigenous communities have widely criticized existing consultation processes as failing to meet ILO 169 intercultural standards, and the Inter-American Court of Human Rights found legal protections insufficient as of 2020. The absence of a dedicated national FPIC or community social legislation law creates significant legal uncertainty and ongoing conflict risk for the Rincón and Sal de Vida projects.

legislation_or_constitution.name: National Constitution of Argentina (1994 Reform) — Article 41
legislation_or_constitution.notes: Enshrines the right to a healthful, balanced environment fit for human development as a collective constitutional right. Enables individual citizens to pursue legal actions for environmental protection affecting broader public interests.

legislation_or_constitution.name: National Constitution of Argentina (1994 Reform) — Article 75, point 17
legislation_or_constitution.notes: Requires the state to recognise the ethnic and cultural pre-existence of indigenous peoples of Argentina and community property rights. Marked a constitutional turning point for indigenous rights in the 1990s.

legislation_or_constitution.name: National Constitution of Argentina (1994 Reform) — Article 124
legislation_or_constitution.notes: Establishes that natural resources belong to the provinces, creating a federal-provincial split in resource governance. Mining policy is set nationally via the Mining Code, but provincial governments retain control over natural resources and local mining oversight.

legislation_or_constitution.name: General Environmental Law (Ley General del Ambiente — LGA)
legislation_or_constitution.notes: Requires environmental impact assessments (EIA) before authorising any activity with significant potential environmental or quality-of-life impacts. EIA triggers FPIC requirements at the exploration stage. Provincial governments designate the responsible public office for EIA analysis and authorisation.

legislation_or_constitution.name: Mining Code (Código de Minería)
legislation_or_constitution.notes: National legislation governing mining policy, enacted by the National Congress. Coexists with provincial regulations. Structured to attract private investment with large tax breaks and limited royalties.

international_conventions_ratified.convention: ILO Convention No. 169 on Indigenous and Tribal Peoples
UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)

global_frameworks_applied.framework: ILO Convention No. 169 — Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: ILO Convention 169 is a legally binding instrument ratified by Argentina. Argentine courts have recognised indigenous peoples' special cultural and spiritual relationship with land as recognised by this Convention. However, in practice, communities in the high Andean salt flats (including the Puna region covering Rincón and Sal de Vida) have not had projects reviewed with them to obtain their consent, have not shared in benefits, nor been compensated for ecosystem damage, constituting a gap between ratification and implementation.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) — Articles 3, 4, 19, 26
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: UNDRIP requires states to consult and cooperate in good faith with indigenous peoples through their own representative institutions to obtain free, prior and informed consent before adopting measures that may affect them. Failures to implement FPIC at Puna region lithium projects directly violate Articles 3, 4, 19, and 26 of UNDRIP.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: IFC Performance Standards (IFC PS) — Salar del Rincón Project
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: The Salar del Rincón Project (Rincón) is linked to IFC financing (IFC Project #49112), indicating IFC Performance Standards apply to the project, including requirements on indigenous peoples, environmental and social assessment, and community engagement.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: IDB Invest Environmental and Social Standards — Sal de Vida Project
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: IDB Invest is associated with the Sal de Vida project, as evidenced by a published Q&A document. IDB Invest applies its own environmental and social framework including FPIC requirements for projects affecting indigenous communities.

human_rights_frameworks.modern_slavery_risk: High — The Salar del Rincón and Sal de Vida projects operate in extremely remote, high-altitude locations (3,725m and 4,100m ASL respectively) in the Puna region, far from urban centres and regulatory oversight, conditions that elevate risks of labour exploitation and coercive recruitment. Argentina's provincial mining governance is structured to attract private investment with limited royalties and large tax breaks, reducing accountability. Indigenous communities in the region have been systematically excluded from FPIC processes, denied benefit-sharing, and not compensated for ecosystem damage despite ILO 169 ratification — indicators of structural marginalisation that correlates with heightened modern slavery risk. The combination of weak enforcement of national minimum standards at the provincial level, geographic isolation, migrant and informal labour patterns common in extractive industries in the Puna, and documented gaps between constitutional protections and on-the-ground implementation collectively support a High risk classification.
human_rights_frameworks.gap_summary: Significant implementation gaps exist between Argentina's strong formal human rights and indigenous rights framework and actual practice at both project sites. Key gaps include: (1) FPIC non-compliance — despite ratification of ILO Convention 169 and endorsement of UNDRIP, indigenous communities in the Puna region (covering both Rincón and Sal de Vida areas) have not had projects reviewed with them, have not obtained free, prior and informed consent, have not shared in project benefits, and have not been compensated for ecosystem damage; (2) Federal-provincial governance tension — while the General Environmental Law sets national minimum EIA and FPIC standards, provincial governments (Salta, Catamarca) control mining oversight and are structured to prioritise investment attraction over environmental and social protection, with limited royalties and tax breaks reducing accountability levers; (3) EIA and FPIC procedural gaps — although EIA is legally required and triggers FPIC at the exploration stage, enforcement and meaningful community participation in EIA processes at remote high-altitude sites is inconsistent; (4) Benefit-sharing absence — no documented mechanisms ensuring indigenous communities receive equitable benefit-sharing from lithium extraction at either project; (5) Modern slavery and labour monitoring gaps — no specific modern slavery legislation or supply chain due diligence law identified in the findings for Argentina; and (6) Ecosystem and water rights — both projects sit in fragile closed-basin ecosystems critical to indigenous livelihoods, with no documented evidence of legally binding water rights or ecosystem protections specific to these salars beyond general constitutional and EIA frameworks.

legislation.name: Ley de Seguridad Interior No. 24.059
legislation.notes: Argentina's foundational national-level security law governing coordination between federal and provincial security forces. Private security operates as a subordinate and complementary activity to public security.

legislation.name: Decree 1002/1999 — Private Security Services Regulatory Framework
legislation.notes: Issued 10 September 1999. Establishes a regulatory framework on the provision of private security services, investigations, surveillance, and custody over persons and/or property at the national level. Applied within the framework of National Weapons and Explosives Law No. 20,429 and Interior Security Law No. 24,059.

legislation.name: National Weapons and Explosives Law No. 20,429
legislation.notes: Referenced in Decree 1002/1999 as part of the overarching framework governing private security services, firearms, and controlled materials in Argentina.

legislation.name: Decree-Law No. 15,385/1944 — Border Security Zone Requirements
legislation.notes: Establishes that acquisition, rental, or lease of real estate in border security zones by foreign companies requires prior government approval. Relevant to Puna operations near Chilean and Bolivian borders. Mining concessions are exempt but surface land transactions are not.

legislation.name: Buenos Aires Province Law 12,297 — Private Security Reference Model
legislation.notes: Reference model only — not applicable to Salta or Jujuy directly. Illustrates consistent principles across Argentine provinces: private security must adhere to reasonableness, graduality, and non-intervention in political/labor/union/religious conflicts. Private security is complementary and subordinate to provincial state security.

legislation.name: Jujuy Decree N°7592/11 — Lithium Strategic Mineral Declaration
legislation.notes: Jujuy Province declared lithium a strategic resource via this decree. Enables provincial state-owned mining companies to hold mining rights and develop them in association with private investors. Has indirect implications for security governance and oversight of mining operations.

security_frameworks.conflict_risk_level: Medium — The Puna region sits along the Argentine-Chilean and Argentine-Bolivian borders, triggering border security zone regulations (Decree-Law 15,385/1944) that add regulatory complexity for foreign-owned operators. The federal/provincial regulatory split creates governance ambiguity, as no standalone consolidated private security law was found for Salta or Jujuy provinces specifically. Lithium's declaration as a strategic resource in Jujuy introduces heightened state interest and potential for labor, indigenous community, or political tensions, which private security personnel are explicitly prohibited from intervening in under Argentine law.
security_frameworks.gap_summary: 1. No publicly available, standalone consolidated private security law was identified for Salta Province or Jujuy Province specifically — operators must contact each province's Secretaría de Seguridad directly to confirm current authorization and licensing requirements. 2. No evidence found of Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights (VPs) being formally adopted or applied by operators in the Rincon or Sal de Vida project areas. 3. The findings were cut off before completing Section 4.1 on Gendarmería Nacional Argentina (GNA), leaving the role of federal border security forces in Puna mining operations undocumented. 4. No information found on specific municipal-level security requirements for the Salar del Rincón (Salta) or Salar del Hombre Muerto (Catamarca/Jujuy border) areas. 5. No company-specific security codes of conduct, third-party audits, or human rights due diligence disclosures were identified in the findings.

safety_equipment_availability.ppe_availability: PPE availability is governed by national Law No. 19,587 and Decree No. 249/2007, which mandate employers to supply and maintain personal protective equipment across all mining operations in Argentina. Rio Tinto, as operator of both Rincon and Sal de Vida, is subject to these obligations. However, the projects are located in remote, high-altitude (3,500–4,000+ m ASL), hyper-arid Puna region locations, which constrains local commercial availability of PPE. No specific local supplier data or PPE stock levels were identified in the findings.
safety_equipment_availability.specialist_equipment_notes: Decree No. 249/2007 mandates mining-specific health and safety services including blast safety, explosives handling by trained personnel, and hygiene and safety programs directed by qualified specialists. The Mining Safety Program must be prepared by a Hygiene and Safety Manager. Occupational Risk Insurers (ARTs) must provide advisory services for safety plan preparation. No specific data on availability of cranes, MEWPs, gas detection equipment, or fall arrest systems at these project sites was identified in the findings. The remote Puna location and high altitude present logistical challenges for specialist equipment access.
safety_equipment_availability.import_feasibility: No specific data on import costs, customs procedures, or lead times for safety equipment was identified in the findings. Argentina operates under national customs regulations, and the projects are subject to the RIGI (Large Investment Incentive Regime) at Rincon, which may offer some import facilitation. The remote location in the Puna region would extend effective lead times for imported equipment beyond standard Argentine import timelines.
known_specific_gaps: No specific local safety equipment suppliers identified in the Puna/Puna adjacent region
No lithium-project-specific safety equipment regulatory framework exists in Argentina; obligations derive only from general mining and occupational safety law
Emergency drill and rescue equipment requirements may vary by province (Salta vs Catamarca) and are not uniformly regulated at national level
High altitude (3,500–4,000+ m ASL) and remote hyper-arid conditions create logistical constraints on equipment availability and maintenance
Annual inspection minimum at provincial level may result in infrequent verification of safety equipment compliance

training_providers.framework_name: Provincial Plan for Sustainable Mining Development 2030 (Education Axis No. 1)
training_providers.mining_specific_training: Specialist mining education is available through UNJu's Escuela de Minas 'Dr. Horacio Carrillo' (offering Técnico Minero, Técnico Químico, and Técnico en Informática specialisations across a 6-year Ciclo Básico/Superior structure), and through the Centro de Formación Virtual Universitario de Olacapato (a partnership between the Salta Government, Municipality of San Antonio de los Cobres, UCASAL, Rio Tinto Rincón Litio, and Puna Mining, active since 2024). Additional technical and entrepreneurship training is delivered by Puna Mining S.A. in Olacapato and Pocitos in partnership with Banco Macro, INTI, and local municipalities. Minera Santa Rita runs structured internship programs at its Campo Quijano and Olacapato industrial plants for secondary and tertiary students. Rio Tinto's Rincón project commits to technical training aligned with advanced processing technologies and quality control systems.
training_providers.gap_summary: No evidence found of internationally recognised certifications (e.g. IOSH, NEBOSH, ICAM, BOSIET) being available locally in the Puna region. Workforce literacy rates for the Puna/NOA corridor are not reported in the findings. Specific curriculum details, graduate output numbers, and training capacity figures for most providers are absent. No registered provider directory or national-level mining training registry is documented. The Sal de Vida project (Catamarca) lacks specific training provider information beyond its geographic proximity to the NOA lithium corridor ecosystem. Details on high-altitude or specialist lithium-processing certifications are not documented.

notable_providers.name: SECURION (SECAR Security Group S.A.)
notable_providers.notes: Largest national private security company with over 30 years of operation in Argentina. ISO 9001:2015 certified. Former Securitas AB affiliate, now a Certified Partner of Securitas for Argentina. Has 9,000 employees and national monitoring infrastructure including satellite monitoring, autonomous robotics, and remote-site mining capability. Operates under NFPA, UL and FM Global standards. A historical bribery complaint was filed against the former Securitas Argentina SA entity; the company was subsequently acquired by Argentine capital and rebranded as Securion, with new management stated to be unconnected to alleged improper payments. No specific contracts confirmed at Rincón or Sal de Vida.

notable_providers.name: Prosegur Argentina
notable_providers.notes: Leading international security company operating in Argentina for over 30 years. Operates an iSOC in Buenos Aires monitoring over 5,000 installations and 600+ customers across banking, mining, oil & gas, energy, logistics, and retail. Manages 15,000 cameras, 350,000+ alarms and GPS signals, and conducts 10,000 virtual rounds per month. Part of a global network of 14 interconnected iSOC centres. Has explicit mining and oil & gas client base documented in Argentina. Provincial licensing in Salta and/or Jujuy would be required for armed on-site operations. No specific contracts confirmed at Rincón or Sal de Vida.

notable_providers.name: G4S Argentina (G4S Soluciones de Seguridad S.A.)
notable_providers.notes: Global Tier 1 security firm with a dedicated mining vertical and extensive experience in mining security across Latin America. Legally registered in Argentina as G4S SOLUCIONES DE SEGURIDAD S.A. (CUIT 30-54948651-3), with activity coding 801090 (Security and Investigation Services). Confirmed present and legally authorised to operate in all provinces of Argentina, including confirmed provincial tax registration in Salta. Most explicitly documented mining specialisation of any national-scale provider in Argentina. No specific contracts confirmed at Rincón or Sal de Vida.

notable_providers.name: Cóndor Seguridad Privada
notable_providers.notes: Local SME based in San Antonio de los Cobres, Salta (Puna Alta). Indigenous-owned. Actively serving Puna mining projects including Ganfeng Lithium and Posco. Identified as the only provider with confirmed on-the-ground presence and active contracts specifically within the Puna mining corridor at the time of research.

security_companies.armed_security_regulation: Regulated: Armed private security in Argentina requires provincial licensing. Providers must obtain authorisation in each province where they operate armed guards. Confirmed that provincial registration in Salta is required for armed on-site operations at projects such as Rincón. G4S is confirmed as legally authorised in all Argentine provinces including Salta. Other national providers such as Prosegur would similarly require provincial licensing in Salta and/or Jujuy to deploy armed personnel.
security_companies.gap_summary: No specific contract evidence was found for any provider at the Rincón (Salta) or Sal de Vida (Catamarca/Jujuy) project sites at the time of research. The research findings for Cóndor Seguridad Privada were incomplete (entry cut off in source material), limiting full assessment of that provider. Human rights certification status (e.g. PSCI, Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights) was not documented for any identified provider. Regulatory detail on Jujuy provincial licensing requirements was not fully elaborated. No evidence was found of ASIS or ISO 18788 certifications for any listed provider.

socioeconomic_context.poverty_rate: Northwest region: 25.4% poverty incidence (highest in Argentina); child poverty in Northwest 52.8% (second half of 2024); Salta Province poverty rates consistently above national average, over 50% in vulnerable rural/indigenous zones
socioeconomic_context.basic_sanitation_access: Access disparities persist in remote Puna zones; indigenous communities rely on mobile units and basic posts amid higher poverty rates; qualitative evidence of inadequate infrastructure in rural and indigenous-majority areas
socioeconomic_context.clean_water_access: Scarce drinking water in Puna/Andes desert region; indigenous communities face water scarcity; region receives less than 400 mm precipitation per year (some areas only ~100 mm/year); water availability is a key ecosystem service at risk
socioeconomic_context.healthcare_system_quality: Remote Puna and Valle Calchaquí zones rely on mobile health units and basic posts; indigenous communities face restricted access to basic services including healthcare; compounded disadvantages including malnutrition reported among indigenous populations
socioeconomic_context.infectious_disease_risk: Not explicitly stated in findings; high-altitude environment (average 4,000 m asl), extreme diurnal temperature variation (~25°C), and arid conditions pose occupational health risks including altitude sickness and dehydration for project workforce
socioeconomic_context.local_workforce_skill_level: Predominantly pastoralism, subsistence agriculture, traditional salt extraction, and seasonal mining labour; some local entrepreneurs run high-mountain logistics companies serving mining firms; limited formal skilled workforce locally, with mining operations drawing workers from broader region
socioeconomic_context.infrastructure_quality: Roads: remote and long distances — Sal de Vida is 650 km from Catamarca city and 390 km from Salta city; San Antonio de los Cobres is ~160 km from Salta city; Power/comms: qualitative evidence of inadequate infrastructure in isolated Puna region; high-mountain logistics companies have developed to serve mining operations
socioeconomic_context.overall_context_summary: The Argentine Puna region hosting the Rincon (Salta) and Sal de Vida (Catamarca) lithium projects is one of the most economically disadvantaged and sparsely populated areas in Argentina, characterised by extreme altitude (~4,000 m asl), hyper-aridity, scarce water, and structural poverty exceeding 25% in the Northwest region with child poverty reaching 52.8%. Indigenous Kolla and Atacameño communities depend on pastoralism, subsistence farming, and ecosystem services from the salares, yet face compounded disadvantages including malnutrition, water scarcity, and limited access to healthcare and basic services. Large-scale lithium investment — including Rio Tinto's USD 2.5 billion Rincon expansion and USD 700 million Sal de Vida project — represents the dominant formal economic driver in the region, though tensions persist around water use, indigenous rights, and equitable benefit-sharing.

overall_compliance_maturity.rag_rationale: Argentina's Puna region (Rincon/Salar del Rincón and Sal de Vida/Salar del Hombre Muerto) presents a mixed compliance maturity picture warranting an AMBER rating. On the positive side, there is a well-established federal mining law framework (Mining Code, Law 24,196, Law 25,675), a clear EIA/EIS permitting process with mandatory community participation requirements, and a new large-investment incentive regime (RIGI, Ley 27,742) providing 30-year fiscal and regulatory stability that has already attracted over USD 42 billion in committed mining investment. However, significant compliance risks and uncertainties persist. Provincial primacy in environmental licensing creates jurisdictional inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca. The April 2026 Glacier Law amendment — transferring protected-area designation from IANIGLA to provincial governments — introduces a structural conflict of interest and has generated legal challenges. The Wetlands Law interaction with Puna salares remains unresolved. Decree 449/25's simplified compliance framework is promising but dependent on the robustness of professional accreditation and liability systems, which have historically encountered implementation friction in Argentina. The extreme remoteness and altitude of operations (approx. 4,000m ASL) adds operational complexity. Overall, the regulatory architecture is present and improving but implementation consistency, recent environmental law changes, and unresolved legal ambiguities prevent a GREEN rating.
key_strengths: Established federal mining legal framework including Mining Code (1887, as amended), Law 24,196, and Law 25,675 providing EIA requirements at all project stages
RIGI regime (Ley 27,742, 2024) offers 30 years of fiscal, customs, and foreign exchange stability for projects committing USD 200M+, with 13 mining projects already approved and USD 42B+ committed
Mandatory community participation in EIA processes across Catamarca, Jujuy, and Salta via provincial resolutions
Clear two-year EIS cycle with mandatory biennial updates providing structured compliance checkpoints
Decree 449/25 introduces a simplified compliance framework for lithium brine projects advancing through exploration to feasibility
Argentina hosts 70–80% of global lithium brine reserves, creating strong institutional and economic incentive to develop and maintain a functional regulatory environment
Strong investor protections and accelerated depreciation under RIGI enhance project bankability
key_barriers: Provincial primacy over mining approvals, environmental licensing, and royalty structures creates jurisdictional inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca — consistency of application cannot be assumed

April 2026 Glacier Law amendment transfers protected-area designation authority from IANIGLA to provincial governments, creating inherent conflict of interest where provinces both benefit financially from mining and determine environmental protections; has generated legal challenges and protests
Wetlands Law interaction with Puna salares remains legally unresolved, creating material regulatory uncertainty for lithium brine operations in salt flat environments
Argentina's regulatory history shows significant implementation friction between well-intentioned legal reforms and on-the-ground compliance effectiveness
Decree 449/25 simplified framework is only as effective as the underlying professional accreditation and liability systems supporting it
Extreme operational environment (approx. 4,000m ASL, remote, arid) adds complexity to HSESC monitoring and enforcement
National government constitutionally limited to setting minimum environmental standards only, leaving substantive oversight to provinces with varying capacity and independence
overall_compliance_maturity.recommended_approach: Documents should be calibrated to reflect an evolving but inconsistent compliance environment. Due diligence materials must be province-specific (Salta for Rincon; Catamarca/Jujuy border for Sal de Vida) rather than relying on national-level frameworks alone. Legal risk sections should prominently flag the Glacier Law amendment's legal uncertainty and ongoing challenges, and the unresolved Wetlands Law ambiguity as material risks requiring ongoing monitoring. RIGI eligibility and benefits should be clearly documented where applicable, as they represent a significant de-risking factor for large-capital projects. EIA/EIS status and community consultation records should be verified at provincial level and tracked on the biennial renewal cycle. Environmental and social compliance sections should note that provincial enforcement capacity and independence may vary and that implementation friction is a documented systemic risk. Water management and ecosystem impact disclosures should be treated with particular care given the hydrological sensitivity of high-altitude salares. All source URLs from findings should be preserved in reference materials.

development_classification.notes: Quebec, Canada is a mature, high-functioning First World OECD jurisdiction with a well-established dual federal-provincial regulatory framework. For HSESC compliance, this means: strong statutory environmental and social protections apply (Quebec Mining Act as reformed by Bill 63 adopted November 2024, and federal Impact Assessment Act 2019); mandatory Indigenous consultation obligations exist under the James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement (JBNQA) and the new Impact Exploration Authorization (ATI) regime effective May 2024; Whabouchi Mine holds all core environmental approvals (JBNQA authorization September 2015, General Certificate of Authorization under CEAA 2012) but is currently in a paused/care-and-maintenance state pending Rio Tinto's strategic evaluation of spodumene supply options (Galaxy vs Whabouchi) expected to conclude in first half of 2026; high standards of regulatory transparency, accountability, and enforceable Indigenous rights apply throughout; HSESC requirements will reflect a high-compliance baseline consistent with Canadian federal and Quebec provincial norms.

legislation.name: Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST / AOHS) — CQLR c S-2.1
legislation.regulator: CNESST (Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail)
legislation.notes: Core provincial OSH legislation applicable to Nemaska Lithium Whabouchi Mine, Bécancour Conversion Facility, and AP60 aluminum smelter. Establishes employer obligations to protect health, safety, and physical and psychological integrity of workers. Amended by Bill 59 (2021) and Bill 42 (2024).

legislation.name: Act Respecting Industrial Accidents and Occupational Diseases (LATMP / AIAOD) — CQLR c A-3.001
legislation.regulator: CNESST (Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail)
legislation.notes: Governs compensation, financing, and rehabilitation for employment injuries. Complements the LSST by dealing with the consequences of occupational accidents and diseases. Applicable to all Quebec operations including Whabouchi Mine, Bécancour, and AP60.

legislation.name: An Act to Modernise the Occupational Health and Safety System (Bill 59) — SQ 2021
legislation.regulator: CNESST (Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail)
legislation.notes: First major reform to Quebec OSH in nearly 40 years, passed September 30, 2021. Final provisions came into force October 1, 2025 via Order in Council 1154-2025. Requires employers with 20+ workers to implement prevention programmes; smaller establishments must maintain action plans. Mandates health and safety committees, registers of contaminants/hazardous substances updated every three years, and integration of psychosocial risk prevention. Applies to all Quebec operations.

legislation.name: An Act to Prevent and Fight Psychological Harassment and Sexual Violence in the Workplace (Bill 42) — SQ 2024, c 4
legislation.regulator: CNESST (Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail)
legislation.notes: Amends the LSST. Assented to 2024-03-27. Strengthens obligations to prevent and address psychological harassment and sexual violence in the workplace. Applicable to all Quebec operations including mining and industrial facilities.

legislation.name: Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines — CQLR c S-2.1, r.14
legislation.regulator: CNESST (Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail)
legislation.notes: Key sector-specific regulation for the Whabouchi Mine site. Applies to both open-pit and underground mining operations. Requires engineer-certified plans and specifications before excavation work. Mandates modular miner training (Modules 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7 of the Centre de services scolaire de l'Or-et-des-Bois program) for all underground mine workers. Sets PPE and site safety requirements.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: ILO Modular Miner Training Standards (referenced via Quebec Mining Association compliance context)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: The Quebec Mining Association (QMA) has established compulsory modular training programs for mine workers aligned with the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines. These training obligations for underground mines reflect internationally recognized competency-based safety training principles.

osh_legislation.gap_summary: The findings do not detail coverage of: (1) federal Canada Labour Code obligations, which may apply to any federally regulated activities on-site; (2) specific environmental legislation (e.g., Quebec Environment Quality Act or federal Impact Assessment Act) governing the Whabouchi Mine or Bécancour facility beyond OSH; (3) Cree Nation / Indigenous land rights and free, prior and informed consent (FPIC) requirements under the James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement or UNDRIP as applied to the Whabouchi Mine in Eeyou Istchee; (4) security-specific legislation or regulations applicable to the facilities; (5) community relations or social licence obligations under Quebec or federal law; (6) specific regulations governing the AP60 aluminum smelter beyond the general provincial OSH framework; and (7) transportation of dangerous goods or hazardous materials regulations applicable to lithium compounds produced at Bécancour.

legislation.name: Mining Act (CQLR, c. M-13.1)
legislation.regulator: Ministère des Ressources naturelles et des Forêts (MRNF)
legislation.notes: Primary statute governing mineral exploration and extraction in Quebec. Governs Nemaska Lithium's Whabouchi Mine and Bécancour Conversion Facility. Requires rehabilitation and restoration plans and financial guarantees for mine closure. Significantly amended by Bill 63 (assented to November 29, 2024), which abolished the 2,000 metric ton BAPE threshold, introduced provisions on critical and strategic minerals, and enabled Indigenous community agreements on land boundaries.

legislation.name: Bill 63 — An Act to amend the Mining Act and other provisions
legislation.regulator: Ministère des Ressources naturelles et des Forêts (MRNF)
legislation.notes: Presented to the National Assembly on May 28, 2024; assented to on November 29, 2024. First significant reform of the Mining Act since 2013. Key changes include: universal environmental assessment for all mining projects (abolishing the 2,000 metric ton BAPE threshold), redesignation of 'claims' as 'exclusive exploration rights', new critical and strategic minerals provisions, Indigenous community agreements on land use, and new conditions for holding mining rights. Draft regulations published March 2026 subject to 45-day public consultation.

legislation.name: Authorization for Impact-Causing Exploration Work (ATI) Regulation
legislation.regulator: Ministère des Ressources naturelles et des Forêts (MRNF)
legislation.notes: Effective May 6, 2024. Requires any mining company conducting 'impact' exploration work to obtain an Authorization for Impact-Causing Exploration Work (ATI). Requires notification to municipal executive directors and First Nations band councils, who have 30 days to submit questions and comments. Applies to exploration activities relevant to Nemaska Lithium's Whabouchi Mine area.

legislation.name: Environment Quality Act (EQA) (CQLR, c. Q-2)
legislation.regulator: Ministère de l'Environnement, de la Lutte contre les changements climatiques, de la Faune et des Parcs (MELCCFP)
legislation.notes: Quebec's principal environmental statute. Requires certificates of authorization under section 22 for certain activities and projects. Establishes the Environmental Impact Assessment and Review Procedure (EIARP), including BAPE public hearings. Title II establishes a special regime for James Bay and Northern Quebec (JBNQA territory) with active Indigenous community involvement — directly applicable to the Whabouchi Mine. Also governs depollution attestations for large industrial establishments such as the AP60 Smelter (Complexe Arvida). The EIARP process can take up to two years.

legislation.name: Regulation respecting mineral substances other than petroleum, natural gas and brine
legislation.regulator: Ministère des Ressources naturelles et des Forêts (MRNF)
legislation.notes: Enacted under the Mining Act. Requires mining companies to submit rehabilitation and restoration plans (closure plans) and provide financial guarantees covering reclamation costs. Applies to both mineral exploration activities and mining operations, including Nemaska Lithium's Whabouchi Mine.

legislation.name: James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement (JBNQA)
legislation.regulator: Government of Quebec / Government of Canada / Cree Nation Government / Makivik Corporation (Inuit)
legislation.notes: Foundational treaty governing the Whabouchi Mine area. An Aboriginal land claim settlement covering economic development, property issues, and cultural, social and governmental institutions for Indigenous peoples in northern Quebec. Establishes the environmental and social impact assessment and review procedure under Title II of the EQA for mining projects in the James Bay and Northern Quebec territory. Bill 5 (Quebec's project acceleration legislation) does not apply to this territory as the JBNQA processes cannot be unilaterally amended.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: Fraser Institute Investment Attractiveness Index
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: Quebec ranks 5th globally on the Fraser Institute's annual survey of mining investment attractiveness, reflecting the maturity and stability of its regulatory framework.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: Bureau d'audiences publiques sur l'environnement (BAPE) Public Hearing Process
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: BAPE public hearings are a mandatory component of Quebec's Environmental Impact Assessment and Review Procedure (EIARP) under the EQA. BAPE recommendations must be taken into account by the Quebec government when deciding whether to authorize or deny a project. Post-Bill 63, all mining projects must undergo this assessment regardless of size.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) — Indirect Application via JBNQA and Bill 63
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: Bill 63 introduced provisions enabling the Government of Quebec to enter into agreements with Indigenous communities (Cree Nation Government, Makivik Corporation, Kativik Regional Government, or individual band councils) to determine land boundaries for mineral withdrawal, and included Indigenous relations training as a potential criterion for holding mining rights. The JBNQA treaty regime under Title II of the EQA ensures active involvement of Cree, Inuit and Naskapi communities in environmental assessment of northern mining projects.

environmental_legislation.gap_summary: The research findings are cut off before completing the section on JBNQA Land Categories (Section 4.1), leaving the details of the land categorization system (Category I, II, and III lands) under the JBNQA unaddressed. No specific details are provided on federal legislation applicable to these projects (e.g., the Impact Assessment Act, Fisheries Act, or Species at Risk Act), which would typically apply alongside provincial legislation. The year of original enactment for the Mining Act (CQLR, c. M-13.1) and the Regulation respecting mineral substances is not explicitly stated in the findings. Details on the specific environmental authorizations and certificates obtained by Nemaska Lithium for the Bécancour Conversion Facility (which falls outside the James Bay territory and therefore under the standard EIARP regime) are not provided. The specific depollution attestation status and conditions for Rio Tinto's AP60 Smelter are not detailed beyond the general legislative framework. Draft regulations under Bill 63 (published March 2026) have not yet come fully into force, creating regulatory uncertainty for future projects.

legislation.name: James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement (JBNQA)
legislation.regulator: Government of Quebec, Government of Canada, Hydro-Québec, Grand Council of the Crees of Quebec, Northern Quebec Inuit Association
legislation.notes: The foundational modern treaty governing land management and Indigenous rights in the James Bay and Northern Quebec region. The Whabouchi mine sits on Category III land under the JBNQA. Establishes environmental and social protection regimes (sections 22, 23, 24) and requires consent of native peoples for mining on Category I lands. Constitutionally protected.

legislation.name: Agreement Respecting a New Relationship Between the Cree Nation and the Government of Quebec (Paix des Braves)
legislation.regulator: Government of Quebec, Government of Canada, Grand Council of the Crees
legislation.notes: Signed February 7, 2002 in Waskaganish. A 50-year agreement providing for revenue sharing and joint management of mining, forestry and hydroelectric resources on traditional Cree lands. Establishes joint jurisdiction between Quebec government and Cree Nation in James Bay municipalities and surrounding territories. Constitutionally protected and prevails over subsequent provincial legislation where conflicts arise.

legislation.name: Agreement on Governance in the Eeyou Istchee James Bay Territory
legislation.regulator: Government of Quebec, Cree Nation, Jamésiens
legislation.notes: Signed July 24, 2012. Builds on the JBNQA and Paix des Braves to establish a partnership between the Cree, Quebec and the Jamésiens for the governance of Eeyou Istchee James Bay Territory.

legislation.name: Act Giving Effect to the Agreement on Cree Nation Governance (Cree Nation Governance Agreement)
legislation.regulator: Government of Canada, Crees of Eeyou Istchee
legislation.notes: Gives effect and force of law to the Agreement on Cree Nation Governance signed July 18, 2017, and its companion the Cree Constitution. Establishes Cree Nation self-governance framework relevant to the territory in which the Whabouchi mine operates.

legislation.name: Environmental Quality Act (Quebec) — Chapter II (COMEX provisions)
legislation.regulator: Ministère de l'Environnement, de la Lutte contre les changements climatiques, de la Faune et des Parcs (MELCCFP); COMEX (Environmental and Social Impact Review Committee)
legislation.notes: Under Chapter II of Quebec's Environmental Quality Act, COMEX — a bipartite Quebec-Cree independent body — is responsible for assessment and review of social and environmental impacts of projects in the JBNQA territory. COMEX reviewed the Whabouchi Project (file 3214-14-052), held public hearings in 2015, and has issued and tracked multiple Certificate of Authorization amendments (MODCA1 through MODCA9+) since 2016.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) — JBNQA Chapter 22 (COMEX Process)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: Nemaska Lithium submitted its impact assessment statement to COMEX in 2013. Public hearings were held in 2015. The process enabled consideration of community concerns and resulted in a Certificate of Authorization for the Whabouchi Project. COMEX is the bipartite Quebec-Cree agency charged with evaluating projects south of the 55th parallel North under the JBNQA framework.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: Canadian Environmental Assessment Act (CEAA) — Federal Environmental Assessment
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: The Whabouchi Mine Project was subject to both the COMEX ESIA process and the Canadian Environmental Assessment Agency (CEAA) review process, as it lies entirely within the JBNQA territory.

global_frameworks_applied.framework: Treaty-Based Indigenous Consent and Revenue Sharing (JBNQA + Paix des Braves)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: The JBNQA and Paix des Braves together require consent for mining on Category I lands, establish revenue sharing, and provide for joint management of natural resources on traditional Cree lands. These treaty frameworks directly governed the approvals and ongoing management of the Whabouchi mine. The Paix des Braves has been cited as a historic model for government-Indigenous relations in Quebec.

community_social_legislation.gap_summary: The research findings focus primarily on the Nemaska Lithium Whabouchi Mine and its JBNQA-governed territory. Specific community and social legislation applicable to the Bécancour Conversion Facility (located in southern Quebec, outside the JBNQA territory) is not addressed in the findings — separate provincial and municipal social/community frameworks would apply there. Similarly, detailed community and social legislation specific to the Rio Tinto AP60 Smelter complex is not covered in these findings. No information is provided on Indigenous Impact and Benefit Agreements (IBAs) or specific financial compensation arrangements, if any, negotiated directly between Nemaska Lithium and the Cree Nation for the Whabouchi project. Details on how the Paix des Braves revenue-sharing provisions apply specifically to lithium mining (as opposed to forestry and hydroelectric resources) are not elaborated upon. The findings also do not address Quebec's Act respecting the rights of persons and families or other provincial social legislation as applied to workers or surrounding communities.

legislation_or_constitution.name: Section 35 of the Constitution Act, 1982
legislation_or_constitution.notes: Constitutionally protects Aboriginal and treaty rights of Indigenous Peoples in Canada; imposes a duty on the Crown to consult and, where appropriate, accommodate Indigenous groups when conduct might adversely impact established or potential Aboriginal or treaty rights. Directly relevant to Whabouchi mine located on Cree treaty lands in Eeyou Istchee.

legislation_or_constitution.name: United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples Act (Bill C-15)
legislation_or_constitution.notes: Received Royal Assent on June 21, 2021. Requires federal law to align with UNDRIP's 46 articles, including free, prior and informed consent (FPIC) for resource extraction on Indigenous lands. Grand Council of the Crees (Eeyou Istchee) was an active supporter. Directly relevant to Whabouchi mine operations on Cree territory.

legislation_or_constitution.name: James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement (JBNQA)
legislation_or_constitution.notes: First modern land claim agreement in Canada; constitutionally protected; defines land categories (I, II, III), resource management rights, environmental and social protection regime for Cree territory. Consent of Indigenous peoples required for mining on Category I lands. Whabouchi mine changes located on JBNQA lands (trapline R20, near R19, Cree Nation of Nemaska). Primary rights framework for the Whabouchi site.

legislation_or_constitution.name: Impact Assessment Act (IAA) / Canadian Environmental Assessment Act (CEAA 2012)
legislation_or_constitution.notes: Whabouchi mine underwent federal environmental assessment under CEAA 2012 (EA Report published July 29, 2015). Subsequent project changes triggered further IAAC review in 2023, including analysis of additional impacts on Indigenous Peoples' rights. IAAC consulted Cree Nation of Nemaska and Cree Nation Government as part of the process.

international_conventions_ratified.convention: United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)

global_frameworks_applied.framework: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) — Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC)
global_frameworks_applied.evidence_of_application: FPIC is embedded in Canada's Bill C-15 (2021), which requires federal law to align with UNDRIP. The Whabouchi mine's environmental and social impact assessment (ESIA) was submitted to both federal (IAAC) and Quebec (COMEX) authorities, with public hearings held in Eeyou Istchee James Bay territory in 2015. The Grand Council of the Crees actively supported Bill C-15. IAAC's 2023 analysis explicitly assessed additional impacts on Indigenous Peoples' rights under the JBNQA.

human_rights_frameworks.modern_slavery_risk: Low — Canada is a mature, high-income jurisdiction with robust federal and provincial labour law, constitutional protections, and active regulatory oversight. The Whabouchi mine and Bécancour plant operate within a heavily regulated environment including JBNQA obligations, IAAC review, and UNDRIP/FPIC requirements. The Rio Tinto AP60 smelter similarly operates under Quebec and federal law. No evidence of forced labour or trafficking risks is present in the findings for these specific operations. However, supply chain risks upstream (e.g., sourcing of other minerals) are not addressed in the available findings and cannot be assessed.
human_rights_frameworks.gap_summary: The findings focus primarily on Indigenous rights frameworks applicable to the Whabouchi mine (JBNQA, Section 35, UNDRIP/Bill C-15) and federal environmental assessment processes. Gaps include: (1) No specific information on Quebec provincial human rights legislation (e.g., Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms) or its application to these operations; (2) No detail on labour rights frameworks or occupational health and safety legislation applicable to the Bécancour processing plant or Rio Tinto AP60 smelter; (3) No information on Canada's ratification status of ILO conventions relevant to Indigenous peoples (e.g., ILO Convention 169), which Canada has not ratified; (4) No specific human rights due diligence or modern slavery reporting obligations identified for these operations under Canadian law; (5) No information on grievance mechanisms or remedy frameworks specific to workers or affected communities at these sites beyond the JBNQA consultation processes.

legislation.name: Impact Assessment Act (IAA) / Canadian Environmental Assessment Act, 2012
legislation.notes: Federal environmental and impact assessment framework governing major projects including Nemaska Lithium's Whabouchi Mine; amended Decision Statement issued September 19, 2023

legislation.name: Explosives Act (Canada)
legislation.notes: Federal legislation regulating manufacture, sale, use, storage and transportation of explosives used in exploration and mining

legislation.name: Explosives Regulations, 2013 (SOR/2024-77 as most recently amended)
legislation.notes: Sets out security requirements for explosives including vehicle locking, secure access-controlled parking, video surveillance, immobilization devices and alarm systems during transport

legislation.name: Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act, 1992
legislation.notes: Federal legislation covering safety and security aspects of transporting explosives and other dangerous goods within Canada

legislation.name: Québec Explosives Act (CQLR, c. E-22)
legislation.notes: Provincial legislation governing explosives in Québec; Sûreté du Québec issues general explosives permits requiring background checks on shareholders with over 10% of shares and special driver's licenses for transport

legislation.name: James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement (JBNQA)
legislation.notes: Governs environmental and social impact assessments in Eeyou Istchee/James Bay territory; authorization granted to Whabouchi mine in September 2015 following COMEX review

legislation.name: Environment Quality Act (Québec)
legislation.notes: Québec provincial legislation under which a Certificate of Authorization was granted for the Whabouchi mining site in September 2015

legislation.name: Loi sur les mines (Mining Act, Québec)
legislation.notes: Québec Mining Act under which Mining Lease number 1022 was granted to Nemaska Lithium Inc. on October 26, 2017, for a 20-year period ending October 25, 2037, covering 138.106 ha

security_frameworks.conflict_risk_level: Low — The Whabouchi Mine is located in the Eeyou Istchee/James Bay region of Québec, Canada, a stable democratic jurisdiction with established Indigenous consultation processes (JBNQA framework). The Bécancour conversion facility and Rio Tinto AP60 smelter at Complexe Arvida (Jonquière/Saguenay) are in established industrial zones. No evidence of armed conflict, significant civil unrest, or elevated security threat was identified in the findings. The mine project was paused in December 2025 due to market/supply chain factors rather than security concerns.
security_frameworks.gap_summary: 1. No site-specific private security policies or contracts for either the Whabouchi Mine, Bécancour facility, or AP60 smelter were identified in the findings. 2. No evidence of Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights (VPs) application was found despite Rio Tinto being a known VP signatory in other jurisdictions. 3. The findings are incomplete — the section on National Critical Infrastructure Protection (Part 2.3) is cut off and no further detail on federal critical infrastructure designation for these assets is available. 4. No specific cybersecurity or physical security management plans for the facilities were referenced. 5. No community security agreements or impact and benefit agreements (IBAs) specific to security provisions with Indigenous communities were cited. 6. The Whabouchi Mine is currently on pause (announced December 2025), creating a gap in current operational security applicability for that site.

safety_equipment_availability.ppe_availability: Quebec operates under a comprehensive OHS framework administered by CNESST, with mandatory PPE requirements enforced through multiple regulations. At Whabouchi Mine, PPE requirements include full body harnesses (CAN/CSA Z259.10 compliant) for fall protection where falls exceed 3m, Class 2 or 3 high-visibility apparel (CSA Z96-09 compliant) for all surface personnel since January 2018, and self-contained breathing apparatus for underground hoistmen with cylinders charged at minimum 13,800 kPa and 6m³ air capacity. At the Rio Tinto AP60 Smelter, the Regulation respecting Safety and Health in Foundry Works (CQLR c. S-2.1, r. 15) mandates specific PPE for smelter/casthouse environments. General workplace PPE and respiratory protection are governed by CQLR c. S-2.1, r. 13.
safety_equipment_availability.specialist_equipment_notes: Fall arrest systems must connect to anchorage systems compliant with CSA Z259.16 or certified per EN 795 or CAN/CSA Z259.15. Self-contained breathing apparatus required for underground hoistmen must maintain positive air pressure at all times inside the facial piece. Ground control and excavation safety requires engineer-certified plans and specifications. Scaling equipment is mandatory for roof, wall, and working face maintenance in underground operations. Autonomous and semi-autonomous vehicle safety equipment requirements were updated via Gazette Officielle du Québec amendment dated October 8, 2025. The remote sub-arctic location of Whabouchi (accessible via all-weather road and Nemiscau airport 18km west) makes on-site stockpiling of specialist equipment critical. Construction phases at Bécancour are subject to the Safety Code for the Construction Industry (CQLR c. S-2.1, r. 4).
safety_equipment_availability.import_feasibility: As a mature Canadian jurisdiction within a CUSMA/USMCA trade framework, import of safety equipment from the US and internationally is generally straightforward with low customs friction. Canadian Standards Association (CSA) certification is required for most safety-critical equipment, which may necessitate recertification or sourcing of CSA-compliant variants. Lead times to the remote Whabouchi site are extended due to sub-arctic location; however, the all-weather road and Nemiscau airport (18km west) provide year-round access. Costs are consistent with Canadian market rates. Emergency resupply to Whabouchi is constrained by remoteness, making local inventory management critical.
known_specific_gaps: Remote sub-arctic location of Whabouchi mine creates resupply challenges in emergencies despite all-weather road and nearby airport access
CSA certification requirements may limit availability of some internationally sourced specialist equipment without recertification

Underground drilling personnel must complete Module 6 of the modular course for miners (Centre de services scolaire de l'Or-et-des-Bois) within 6 months of hiring; until trained, workers must be accompanied by certified personnel, creating potential temporary workforce constraints
Regulatory updates for autonomous/semi-autonomous vehicles (October 2025 Gazette amendment) may require procurement of newly specified equipment not yet widely stocked locally

training_providers.framework_name: Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST / OHSA), CQLR c S-2.1, administered by CNESST, with permanent regime under Act to Modernize the Occupational Health and Safety Regime (AMOHSR, Bill 59) effective October 1, 2025
training_providers.mining_specific_training: Quebec provides mining-specific OHS training through APSM (Association Paritaire pour la Santé et la Sécurité du Travail du Secteur Minier), which delivers in-house, regional, and online training in French and English. The mandatory modular mine worker training program (Formation Modulaire du Travailleur Minier / FMTM) covers 12 modules including working at heights, ground consolidation, explosives, and health and safety regulations, and is compulsory for all workers in underground mines. Mine rescue training is coordinated via CATAMINE in partnership with CNESST. Nemaska Lithium uses PAÉLO (powered by Global Formation), a SaaS digital training platform with 3D immersive environments tailored to the mining, manufacturing, and chemical sectors, for its Bécancour processing plant. The Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines (CQLR c S-2.1, r 14) governs mine-specific training requirements.
training_providers.gap_summary: No data found on internationally recognised certifications (e.g. IOSH, NEBOSH, ICAM, BOSIET) being available or required in this Quebec mining context. Workforce literacy rate is not provided in the findings. Training details specific to the Rio Tinto AP60 Smelter (Complexe Arvida/Jonquière) are not covered in the findings beyond general Quebec regulatory applicability. Indigenous community-specific training programs linked to consultation obligations under the Quebec Mining Act are referenced but not detailed. Information on cost, duration, and scheduling of APSM and FMTM modules is not fully elaborated.

notable_providers.name: GardaWorld
notable_providers.notes: GardaWorld has a documented case study referencing Rio Tinto Aluminerie Quebec, suggesting a prior or existing security services relationship with Rio Tinto aluminium operations in Quebec. No direct confirmation of contracts at Whabouchi Mine, Bécancour, or AP60 Smelter specifically found in public sources.

security_companies.armed_security_regulation: regulated — Quebec and Canada have established provincial and federal frameworks governing private security; however, specific contract and provider details for Nemaska Lithium sites are not publicly disclosed due to explicit confidentiality policy. Security operations at Whabouchi Mine are subject to additional obligations under the James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement (JBNQA) and the Chinuchi Agreement with the Cree Nation, which may influence security provider selection and conduct.
security_companies.gap_summary: 1) Nemaska Lithium explicitly states its supplier list is not publicly disclosed for confidentiality reasons, meaning no confirmed security contractor for Whabouchi Mine or Bécancour Conversion Facility can be identified from public sources. 2) The identity of the security provider(s) at the AP60 Smelter (Complexe Arvida/Jonquière) is not confirmed in any available public source. 3) GardaWorld's case study references Rio Tinto Aluminerie Quebec but does not confirm a current or site-specific contract at AP60 or any Nemaska site. 4) Indigenous procurement obligations under the Chinuchi Agreement and Nemaska Lithium's Buy Local Policy may significantly influence security contractor selection, but no Cree-owned or Indigenous security firm has been publicly identified as a provider. 5) The AP60 smelter expansion only commenced commissioning in March 2026, meaning security procurement for the expanded facility may be recent and undocumented publicly.

socioeconomic_context.infrastructure_quality: The Whabouchi property is located approximately 30 km east of Nemaska and accessible via km 276 on the Route du Nord, approximately 300 km north-northwest of Chibougamau; no broader infrastructure quality assessment provided in findings
