socioeconomic_context.overall_context_summary: Quebec is a mature federal–provincial mining jurisdiction governed by the Mining Act and the James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement, with dual federal (IAAC) and provincial (COMEX) regulatory oversight. The Nemaska Lithium Whabouchi project has experienced significant instability including a 2019 bankruptcy, CAD $1.2 billion in public financing, a mine pause in December 2025, and a roughly 50% workforce reduction at the Bécancour conversion plant, which is approximately 70% complete and targeting first production in 2028. Governance concerns include contested Indigenous consultation obligations, a Quebec Superior Court ruling against the province's free-entry mining regime, and an Auditor General critique of opaque public financing practices.

overall_compliance_maturity.rag_rationale: Quebec presents a mature, well-regulated mining jurisdiction with comprehensive multi-layered permitting under both federal and provincial frameworks, making it broadly compliant but with notable evolving uncertainties. Key indicators of amber status include: (1) Quebec ranked 22nd in the 2024-2025 Fraser Institute investment attractiveness index, falling out of the top ten for the second consecutive year after four years in the top ten, reflecting growing industry concern about regulatory complexity and burden; (2) the Supreme Court of Canada's October 2023 ruling that several provisions of the federal Impact Assessment Act were unconstitutional has introduced legal uncertainty in how federal and provincial oversight interact; (3) Bill 63 (Royal Assent November 29, 2024) introduced significant reforms to the Mining Act — including mandatory provincial environmental assessment for all new mining projects — but additional implementing regulations remain outstanding as of March 2026 draft consultation phase, meaning the regime is not yet fully operationalized; (4) the new ATI exploration authorization requirement introduced May 2024 adds a layer of regulatory process that was previously absent; and (5) Bill 5 (tabled December 2025) aims to accelerate strategic projects but intersects with James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations, creating further complexity. Counterbalancing these concerns, Nemaska Lithium's Whabouchi Mine has obtained all primary authorizations (JBNQA authorization September 2015, federal permit July 2015, provincial Certificate of Authorization September 2015), and Quebec's overall regulatory environment remains structured, transparent and investor-familiar. The combination of strong foundational compliance with material ongoing regulatory evolution and declining investment perception metrics justifies an Amber rather than Green rating.
key_strengths: Quebec is a mature, well-established mining jurisdiction with one of the most comprehensive multi-layered permitting systems globally, operating under both federal and provincial legal frameworks

Nemaska Lithium's Whabouchi Mine has obtained all primary authorizations required to commence mining operations, including JBNQA authorization (September 2015), federal permit under the Canadian Environmental Assessment Act (July 2015), and provincial Certificate of Authorization under the Environment Quality Act (September 2015)
Quebec ranked 5th globally on overall investment attractiveness in 2023 per the Fraser Institute, reflecting historically strong regulatory predictability

Bill 63 (Royal Assent November 29, 2024) represents a significant and industry-supported reform of the Mining Act, introducing greater transparency, improved land use coordination, enhanced environmental requirements and stronger Indigenous community protections

The new ATI authorization requirement (May 2024) formalizes and streamlines the Indigenous and community consultation process for mineral exploration, replacing a previously uncertain and cumbersome informal process

Quebec's focus on critical minerals and EV battery supply chain, including the Nemaska Lithium ecosystem and Rio Tinto AP60 facility, positions it as a strategically important and investor-attractive jurisdiction
MELCCFP has made significant efforts to respond to proponent comments following implementation of new EQA ministerial authorization forms in late 2021
key_barriers: Quebec fell out of the top ten most attractive jurisdictions for investment in both 2024 and 2025 per the Fraser Institute, after four consecutive years in the top ten, signaling growing investor concern about regulatory burden and uncertainty
Quebec has had the third-largest decline in investment attractiveness from 2018 to 2025, dropping from nearly 90.0 to just 74.1 on the Fraser Institute index

The Supreme Court of Canada's October 2023 ruling that several provisions of the federal Impact Assessment Act were unconstitutional has created ongoing legal uncertainty in how federal oversight interacts with provincial assessment regimes

Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, with draft regulations subject to 45-day public consultation, meaning the reformed Mining Act regime is not yet fully operationalized

Bill 5 (tabled December 2025), while aimed at accelerating strategic projects, intersects with James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations and Title II of the EQA, creating potential complexity for projects in affected territories

The introduction of mandatory provincial environmental assessment (including BAPE review) for all new mining projects under Bill 63 adds procedural requirements and timelines for future project phases

The new ATI requirement for impact-causing exploration work (effective May 2024) introduces an additional permitting layer that did not previously exist, adding regulatory steps for exploration-stage activities
overall_compliance_maturity.recommended_approach: Documents for this jurisdiction should be calibrated to reflect an evolving compliance landscape anchored in strong foundational legal structures but subject to material regulatory transition. Due diligence materials should clearly distinguish between (1) legacy authorizations already obtained under prior regulatory frameworks (e.g., Whabouchi's 2015 permits) and (2) forward-looking compliance obligations under the reformed regime post-Bill 63. All documents should explicitly address the unresolved constitutional uncertainty arising from the Supreme Court's October 2023 Impact Assessment Act ruling and its implications for federal-provincial jurisdiction. Permitting timelines and cost estimates should incorporate the new mandatory provincial environmental assessment procedure for all new mining projects, including potential BAPE review. Documents touching on exploration activities must account for the new ATI authorization requirement effective May 2024. Any activities in or near territories subject to the James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement should be flagged for heightened Indigenous consultation obligations, particularly in light of Bill 5's interaction with JBNQA. Given the declining Fraser Institute rankings, investor-facing documents should contextualize this trend against Quebec's structural strengths and strategic critical minerals positioning. Compliance monitoring provisions should be calibrated to track ongoing implementation of Bill 63's outstanding regulations, expected through 2026.
